1: šilḥû-ʾayil ʾereṣ — "send the tribute ram/lamb of the land" — the tribute request to the ruler of the land (Jerusalem?). Sela — the rock city, probably Petra; the request goes from a Moabite place of safety through the desert (midbar) toward the mount of the daughter of Zion. Whether this is diplomatic tribute-seeking or a call for Moab to submit to Judah is debated.
2: ʿôp-nōdēd — "a fluttering/wandering bird" — the simile for Moabite women refugees. nāšîm Môʾāḇ — the women of Moab — are specifically mentioned, emphasizing the vulnerability of the civilian population. The fords (maʿbĕrôt) of the Arnon represent the escape route.
3: The appeal for help: ʿăśî ṣēl — "make shade/shadow" — protection from the midday heat; habbîʾî — "bring in/hide" — the verb for sheltering refugees. The request is addressed in the imperative to an unnamed figure (Jerusalem or its ruler). The phrase ṭallēlê niddāḥîm — "shelter the scattered/driven away ones" — echoes Deut 30:4 and the Isaiah 27:13 vision of the exiles gathered.
4: yāgûrû — "let the outcasts of Moab sojourn with you." The appeal for sanctuary uses the language of covenant hospitality (gûr, to sojourn). mēṣ — oppressor; šōd — devastation; rômēs — the one who tramples. All three are nouns for violence. When these pass (kāllâ), the oracle promises safety. The temporal condition is eschatological — "when violence is gone."
5: hûkan bĕḥesed kissēʾ — "a throne will be established in steadfast love/faithfulness" — the messianic note in the oracle. ḥesed (H2617) — the covenant faithfulness/loyalty/love of YHWH (cf. 55:3). The throne of ḥesed will be occupied by one who judges rightly, seeks justice (dōrēš mišpāṭ), and acts quickly in righteousness (mĕhîr ṣedeq). The phrase anticipates the Davidic king of Isa 9:7 and 11:1-5, and ultimately the messianic king of NT expectation.
6: gāʾôn (H1347) — pride; the root word for Moab's characteristic sin throughout the oracles (15:1-16:14). gēʾâ and gāʾôn — repeated synonyms for pride; ʿebrātô — his fury/wrath. Moab's pride is the cause of his downfall (cf. the Proverbs pattern: "Pride goes before destruction," Prov 16:18). badîm lōʾ kēn — his boastings are not reliable/true.
7: All Moab wails for its raisin-cakes (ʾăšîšê Qîr-ḥāreśet) — the specific ritual foods associated with Moabite festivals (cf. Hos 3:1 where raisin-cakes are associated with Canaanite worship). The grieving is communally focused on specific cultural practices that will be lost. mōgey neḵēʾîm — "stricken/crushed men" is the condition of Moab as she laments what was.
8: The fields of Heshbon and the vine of Sibmah (gephen Sibmâh) — Moabite viticulture is famous in antiquity; Numbers 32:38 and Jer 48:32 both reference Sibmah's vines. The great ones of the nations have beaten down (hālĕmû) the prime branches that extended to Jazer and spread toward the desert — a vivid image of destroyed agricultural prosperity.
9: Isaiah's continued lament — "I will weep as Jazer weeps" — is extraordinary: the prophet participates in the mourning of a foreign nation. šibbolê qayiṣ — "the summer fruit branches"; the harvest of the vineyard will not be celebrated. This compassionate stance toward a nation under judgment models an aspect of prophetic ministry that transcends nationalistic celebration of enemies' defeats.
10: śimḥâ wĕgîl — "joy and gladness" removed from the fruitful land. The wine-press treading (darāḵ) with its traditional shouting (hêdād) falls silent. hišbattî — "I (YHWH) have stopped" — a divine first-person action: the cessation of harvest joy is the direct act of the LORD, not merely a natural consequence. YHWH directly removes the song of the treader.
11: Isaiah's lament deepens: kinnôr (H3658) — lyre/harp. The prophet's inner being (meʿay, bowels/inner parts — the seat of deep emotion in Hebrew anthropology) resonates like a lyre for Moab. lĕQîr-ḥāreś — for Qir-hareseth (another name for a key Moabite city). The prophetic compassion here is deeply personal — not performed but felt in the body.
12: Despite Moab's weeping on the high places (bāmôt) and its prayers in the sanctuary (miqdāš), he will not prevail (lōʾ yûkal). The futility of religious activity in the face of divine judgment is a recurring Isaianic motif (cf. 1:11-15; 58:1-7). True worship cannot coexist with injustice and pride.
13: haddābār ʾăšer dibber Yhwh — "this is the word that YHWH spoke" — the authentication formula confirming the oracle's divine origin. mēʾāz — "long ago/from that time" — suggesting the Moab oracle is not new but a restatement of long-established divine intention. This is editorial framing that situates the oracle in Isaiah's larger collection.
14: bišlōš šānîm kišnê śākîr — "within three years, as the years of a hired worker" — the temporal precision is unusual. The comparison to a hired worker's years (śākîr) means counting exactly, without any rounding or grace period. The glory of Moab (kĕbôd Môʾāb) will be contemptible (nibzeh), and a very small and feeble remnant will remain. This temporal limit both threatens and limits — judgment is real but bounded.
